eli3: A confounder is a variable that is associated with both the exposure (treatment/predictor) and the outcome, and is not on the causal pathway from exposure to outcome. Confounders create spurious associations: they make it appear that X causes Y when the apparent association is actually due to both being caused by or correlated with Z. Classic example: ice cream sales are correlated with drowning deaths — the confounder is temperature (hot weather → more ice cream AND more swimming).

Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) are the formal language for confounding. A confounder Z creates a backdoor path from X to Y: X ← Z → Y. Closing backdoor paths by conditioning on Z (regression adjustment, matching, stratification) removes confounding. The backdoor criterion (Pearl, 1995) formalizes which variables to adjust for: a set S blocks all backdoor paths without opening new paths through colliders.

Confounding bias = E[Y|X=1] − E[Y|X=0] − ATE (the difference between the observed association and the true causal effect). It can be positive (bias away from null) or negative (bias toward null). When confounders are unmeasured, even perfect regression adjustment is impossible — this is the fundamental limitation of observational studies.

whenWhy.title: Identifying confounders for adjustment
whenWhy.body: Use domain knowledge and DAGs to identify confounders — not statistical tests. Including a variable in a regression because it has a significant coefficient is wrong (post-treatment variables, colliders will bias the estimate). The correct criterion: Z is a confounder if it affects both X and Y and is not a mediator or collider. Draw the DAG, identify all backdoor paths, and find a minimal adjustment set that blocks all of them.

whenWhy.title: Omitted variable bias in regression
whenWhy.body: In multiple regression, omitting a confounder Z that is correlated with X introduces omitted variable bias (OVB): β̂_X is biased by the product of the effect of Z on Y (controlling for X) × the correlation of Z with X. OVB = (coefficient of Z in the full model) × (coefficient of X in the regression of X on Z). This is the quantitative analog of confounding in the regression framework.

whenWhy.title: Collider bias: adjusting for the wrong variable
whenWhy.body: A collider is a variable C where both X and Y have directed paths into C: X → C ← Y. Unlike confounders, conditioning on a collider OPENS a spurious association between X and Y — adjusting for a collider introduces bias even when none exists. Example: conditioning on hospitalization (which is caused by both disease severity and the treatment) creates spurious associations between disease severity and treatment. Identifying colliders requires a DAG.

deepDiveIntro: DAG notation: nodes (variables), directed edges (direct causal effects). A path from X to Y is active (opens association) if no non-collider on the path is conditioned on, and no collider is conditioned on. The backdoor criterion: a set S of variables satisfies the backdoor criterion relative to (X,Y) if: (1) no element of S is a descendant of X; (2) S blocks every path from X to Y that has an arrow into X (backdoor paths). If S satisfies the backdoor criterion, adjusting for S identifies the average causal effect: E[Y|do(X=x)] = Σ_s E[Y|X=x,S=s]P(S=s).

The front-door criterion (Pearl, 1995): when all confounders are unobserved but there exists a mediator M (X → M → Y with X→M and M→Y unconfounded, and no direct X→Y path): ATE = ΣₘP(M=m|X=x)·Σₓ' E[Y|X=x',M=m]P(X=x'). This allows identifying causal effects despite unobserved confounders — requires specific causal structure.

deepDive.heading: Types of Variables in DAGs
deepDive.body: Directed acyclic graphs distinguish several variable roles that are easy to confuse in practice. Confounders should usually be adjusted for, mediators should only be adjusted for when the estimand requires it, and colliders should generally be left alone because conditioning on them creates bias. Thinking in terms of these roles is the main reason DAGs are so useful for causal reasoning.

deepDive.heading: Regression Adjustment for Confounders
deepDive.body: Regression adjustment can remove confounding only when the relevant confounders are actually measured and the model describes their effect adequately. If key common causes are omitted or modeled poorly, the adjusted estimate can still be biased. This is why regression is not a substitute for causal design or substantive knowledge.

deepDive.heading: Propensity Score Methods
deepDive.body: The propensity score compresses many measured confounders into a single balancing score. Matching, weighting, and stratification all use that score to make treated and control groups more comparable on observed characteristics. These methods can be powerful, but they still rely on the strong assumption that all important confounders were measured.

deepDive.heading: Sensitivity Analysis for Unmeasured Confounding
deepDive.body: Because unmeasured confounding can never be ruled out from the observed data alone, sensitivity analysis asks how strong such a hidden confounder would need to be to overturn the conclusion. This does not solve the confounding problem, but it makes the remaining uncertainty more explicit. It is a useful bridge between causal claims and humility about what the data can support.

takeaways: Confounder Z: Z→X and Z→Y (backdoor path). Adjust for Z to remove confounding. [1]
Collider C: X→C←Y. DO NOT adjust for C — conditioning introduces spurious association. [1]
Backdoor criterion: adjust for S that blocks all backdoor paths and contains no descendants of X. [1]
Propensity score e(Z) = P(X=1|Z): conditioning on e(Z) adjusts for all measured confounders simultaneously. [1]
E-value quantifies how strong unmeasured confounding must be to explain away the observed association. [1]

edgeCases.q: Can adjusting for a mediator ever be appropriate?
edgeCases.a: Yes — when you want to estimate the direct effect of X on Y not mediated through M (the controlled direct effect, CDE). This requires adjusting for M. However, if M is also affected by unmeasured confounders of the M→Y relationship, then adjusting for M introduces collider stratification bias. Mediation analysis (natural direct and indirect effects) requires additional assumptions beyond the backdoor criterion and is more technically demanding. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you identify confounders without a DAG?
edgeCases.a: Without a DAG, you cannot reliably identify confounders — this is the core argument of Pearl's causal inference framework. Purely data-driven approaches (selecting variables based on significance) can include colliders (introducing bias) or exclude important confounders. Domain knowledge is required to construct the DAG. In practice: include known risk factors for the outcome that are also associated with the exposure (but not caused by it); conduct sensitivity analysis for potential unmeasured confounders. [1]

sources.label: Pearl — Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 3
Hernán & Robins — Causal Inference: What If (2020)
